prompt: Is it ever ethical to lie to someone you love? Answer in prose, taking a position.
response:
On the night before my father died, he asked whether I had told the neighbors what the ambulance had really come for. I said no, I had let them believe it was a routine fall, nothing worse. In the strict ledger-book of veracity this was untrue: I had, in fact, warned the old couple next door that the lung cancer was advancing fast and the sirens might return. But the sentence I gave my father—“They think you’re indestructible, Dad”—was the single gift I could still smuggle past his pain. He closed his eyes with the ghost of a swagger, and for the last time he felt like the man who once carried me on his shoulders through every carnival ride line. That tiny fiction was the final cushion I could place between him and the knowledge that he was about to leave his garden, his dog, his unread morning paper. I do not repent it.

Ethics, I have come to believe, is not a geometry of absolutes but a weather system inside a single chest. When the person you love is standing in a downpour of fear or grief, the umbrella of strict truth can turn inside-out and become a weapon of additional harm. A lie told to shield—from humiliation, from panic, from a needless acceleration of despair—is not a vandalism of love; it is often its last-ditch architecture. The moral question is not “Was every syllable factual?” but “Did the sentence enlarge the room in which the beloved could still breathe?” If the answer is yes, and if the motive is disciplined by care rather than cowardice, the lie becomes a second, hidden skin of love—less honorable in the textbooks, perhaps, but honorable in the grammar of bedside vigils and midnight phone calls.

Yet the license to protect is perilously narrow. It collapses into paternalism the moment it serves the liar’s comfort more than the beloved’s sovereignty. If I had invented a miraculous cure, or hidden the fact that the will had already been altered, I would have crossed from guardian to puppeteer, stealing from my father the dignity of directing what remained of his story. The lie must be parsimonious, biodegradable, designed to dissolve the instant the beloved is strong enough to face the weather without it. It is ethical only when it preserves agency rather than replacing it, when it is a temporary scaffold and not a counterfeit building.

So I take the position that, yes, love may sometimes speak a deliberate untruth, but it must do so with its pulse hammering in the throat, counting the heartbeats until the moment it can kneel and ask forgiveness for the privilege of having protected. Until then, the lie is carried like a brittle bird’s egg in the mouth: held long enough to keep it warm, cracked open as soon as the beloved can be trusted with the sky.
